coredata.dc:title: Engineered DR/NIR dual-emission carbonized polymer dots for simultaneous tracking of lipid droplets and lysosomes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

coredata.dc:creator: Miao, Shan
Sun, Junyong
Li, Ying
Zhang, Qiang
Chen, Hongqi
Gao, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Developing near-infrared fluorescent probes for simultaneous tracking of lipid droplets (LDs) and lysosomes is highly desirable for studying cell metabolism. In this work, deep-red/near-infrared dual-emission carbonized polymer dots (DN-CPDs) were prepared for ratiometric monitoring of the intracellular polarity. Detailed structural analysis revealed that the deep-red emission and near-infrared peak of DN-CPDs originate from the molecular state and surface state, respectively. The surface-state emission was derived from the intraparticle charge-transfer (ICT) effect of the donor-bridge-acceptor (D-π-A) structure of DN-CPDs. The obtained DN-CPDs exhibited excellent dual-labeling ability, large Stokes shifts, ratiometric polarity sensitivity, high selectivity, and satisfactory photostability. Moreover, with the polarity distinction between LDs and lysosomes, DN-CPDs nanoprobes were successfully used to observe the dynamic changes of the two aforementioned organelles during starvation-induced lipophagy and drug-induced lipophagy inhibition processes. This work not only provides a useful tool for LD-lysosome related studies but is also valuable for the preparation of CPDs with long wavelength emission.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Polarity probe
Lipid droplets
Lysosomes
Lipophagy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell culture, cytotoxicity assay, and colocalization experiment
Fluorescence response of DN-CPDs towards polarity
Simultaneous tracking of LDs and lysosomes during starvation-induced lipophagy and drug-induced lipophagy inhibition

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure and morphological characteristics of DN-CPDs: (A) Particle size distribution of DN-CPDs; Inset: TEM image and HRTEM image of DN-CPDs; (B) XRD pattern of DN-CPDs; (C) Raman spectra of DN-CPDs. (D) FT-IR spectra of TBO, TBO-CDs, and DN-CPDs; (E) XPS survey spectrum of DN-CPDs; (F) High-resolution XPS C1s spectra of DN-CPDs; (H) High-resolution XPS O1s spectra of DN-CPDs; (I) High-resolution XPS N1s spectra of DN-CPDs; (J) High-resolution XPS S2p spectra of DN-CPDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission of DN-CPDs in different solvents; (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of DN-CPDs in Diox/H2 O media with different water fractions (fw); (C) The relationship between I801 /I655 and water fractions (fw) in Diox/H2 O media; (D) Fluorescence lifetimes of DN-CPDs in Diox/H2 O media with different water fractions (fw); (E) The relative fluorescence intensity (I655 /I801) of DN-CPDs toward various interfering analytes (100 μM) in PBS (pH 7.4, 20 mM). 1. Blank; 2. Ba2+ ; 3. Ca2+ ; 4. Cu2+ ; 5. Fe3+ ; 6. Hg2+ ; 7. Mg2+ ; 8. NO2 − ; 9. NO3 − ; 10. Cl− ; 11. ClO− ; 12. GSH; 13. L-gly; 14. L-his; 15. L-phe; 16. L-pro; 17. L-iso; 18. Diox; (F) Fluorescence intensity variation of DN-CPDs in Diox/H2 O media with 60 % water fractions at 655 nm and 801 nm over time.

(A) 3D fluorescence spectra of dual-emission DN-CPDs; (B) Schematic illustration of the surface state PL mechanism of DN-CPDs. S0 : ground state, S1 : first excited state, LE: localized excited, Abs: absorbance; (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of DN-CPDs with and without BSA passivation; (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of DN-CPDs at different concentrations.
Fluorescent image of HeLa cells incubated with DN-CPDs in starvation-induced lipophagy. λex = 488 nm. DR channel: λem = 630 − 680 nm; NIR channel: λem = 750 − 800 nm. Scale bar: 25 μm.
Fluorescence image of HeLa cells stained with DN-CPDs after treatment with 100 μM chloroquine. λex = 488 nm. DR channel: λem = 630–680 nm; NIR channel: λem = 750–800 nm. Scale bar: 25 μm.
Schematic illustration of the preparation and application of DN-CPDs.

head.ce:title: Engineered DR/NIR dual-emission carbonized polymer dots for simultaneous tracking of lipid droplets and lysosomes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Provincial Engineering Research Center for Polysaccharide Drugs and Institute of Synthesis and Application of Medical Materials, Department of Pharmacy, Wannan Medical College, Wuhu 241002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Provincial Engineering Research Center for Polysaccharide Drugs and Institute of Synthesis and Application of Medical Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Anhui Provincial Engineering Research Center for Polysaccharide Drugs and Institute of Synthesis and Application of Medical Materials, Department of Pharmacy, Wannan Medical College, Wuhu 241002, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing near-infrared fluorescent probes for simultaneous tracking of lipid droplets (LDs) and lysosomes is highly desirable for studying cell metabolism. In this work, deep-red/near-infrared dual-emission carbonized polymer dots (DN-CPDs) were prepared for ratiometric monitoring of the intracellular polarity. Detailed structural analysis revealed that the deep-red emission and near-infrared peak of DN-CPDs originate from the molecular state and surface state, respectively. The surface-state emission was derived from the intraparticle charge-transfer (ICT) effect of the donor-bridge-acceptor (D-π-A) structure of DN-CPDs. The obtained DN-CPDs exhibited excellent dual-labeling ability, large Stokes shifts, ratiometric polarity sensitivity, high selectivity, and satisfactory photostability. Moreover, with the polarity distinction between LDs and lysosomes, DN-CPDs nanoprobes were successfully used to observe the dynamic changes of the two aforementioned organelles during starvation-induced lipophagy and drug-induced lipophagy inhibition processes. This work not only provides a useful tool for LD-lysosome related studies but is also valuable for the preparation of CPDs with long wavelength emission.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lipid droplets (LDs) are crucial organelles for storing lipids and energy within cells, consisting mainly of an inner core of neutral lipids and a monolayer of phospholipids encapsulated in the outer layer [1,2]. Additionally, they are involved in a variety of pathological and physiological processes, including the stabilization of cellular energetic homeostasis, enzyme-based catalysis, prevention of lipotoxicity and apoptosis. As highly dynamic organelles, LDs are closely associated with other organelles [3]. As an example, the lysosomal involvement in the intracellular degradation process of lipid droplets, called lipophagy, is associated with metabolic diseases or inflammation [4,5]. For the observation of the lipophagy process, it is necessary to monitor both lipid droplets and lysosomes. Many studies have shown that lipid droplets are low-polarity organelles while lysosomes exhibit significantly higher polarity than lipid droplets [6–9]. To this end, developing effective polarity-sensitive tools is expected to achieve simultaneous monitoring of LDs and lysosomes.

Fluorescence-based imaging, due to the merits of nondestructive, high spatiotemporal resolution and high sensitivity, has been widely used for studying numerous dynamic processes and target changes in cells and in vivo [10,11]. Despite the development of numerous lipid droplet- or lysosome-targeted probes in recent years, only a few fluorescent probes that can target both of these organelles have been reported [12,13]. In addition, most of the reported polarity probes emit fluorescence in relatively short wavelength regions (＜650 nm) [14–16], which often suffer from limited tissue penetration depth and low bioimaging resolution due to the absorption and scattering of tissues. Constructing probes with emission in the biological transparency window (650–1700 nm), ranging from the deep-red (DR) (650–700 nm) to the near-infrared (NIR) region (700–1700 nm), can effectively address the aforementioned challenges [17]. In 2023, Prof. Feng and co-workers reported a NIR dicyanoisophorone-based fluorescent probe for high-fidelity imaging of LD polarity in the ferroptosis process and nonalcoholic fatty liver disease tissues [18]. Despite these progresses, it is rarely reported for polarity monitoring in the DR/NIR region with ratiometric probes, which can minimize inaccuracy by measuring the relative change of two fluorescence peaks. On the other hand, most small-molecule fluorescent probes often suffer from photobleaching. Therefore, designing DR/NIR ratiometric fluorescent probes with favorable photostability for dual-targeted imaging of LDs and lysosomes is highly desirable.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), consisting of a polymer framework shell and a graphitized carbon core, are emerging as the transition state materials between polymer dots and fully carbonized carbon dots [19,20]. Benefiting from favorable photostability, low toxicity, chemical inertness, easy modification, and low-cost characteristics, CPDs have shown great potential for application in fluorescent sensing, bio-imaging, optoelectronic devices, and anti-counterfeiting fields in recent years [21–23]. In general, CPDs have abundant functional groups on the surface, including electron-donating groups and electron-accepting groups, which readily form typical donor-bridge-acceptor (D-π-A) structures by incorporating π conjugated carbonized core. The unique D-π-A structure not only endows CPDs with long wavelength or even NIR emission but also responds to polarity through the intraparticle charge-transfer (ICT) mechanism [24,25]. In addition, besides surface states, CPDs also possess molecular state and carbon core state emission centers, which provide more flexible strategies for the preparation of multicolor fluorescent materials and ratiometric fluorescent probes [26]. Thus, CPDs are expected to be used to construct ratiometric probes with long-wavelength emission and applied to intracellular polarity imaging.

Toward this end, DR/NIR dual-emission CPDs (DN-CPDs) with LDs/lysosomes dual-targeting were synthesized via the hydrothermal method. As shown in Scheme 1 , the toluidine blue O (TBO) acts as not only a molecular state emission center but also LDs/lysosomes targeting unit due to its high lipophilic CH3 group and hydrophilic amino residues, while poly(styrene-maleic anhydride) (PSMA) is used as a surface state modulator. The DN-CPDs exhibit DR/NIR dual-emission, dual-targeting of LDs and lysosomes, excellent selectivity, satisfying photostability, and low toxicity. Cellular experiments showed that the prepared DN-CPDs can be applied as an effective tool for understanding lipophagy processes.

Unless otherwise noted, all commercial reagents were used without further purification. Toluidine blue O (TBO) was purchased from Adamas and amphiphilic poly(styrene-co-maleic anhydride) (PSMA) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Lyso-Tracker green was purchased from Shanghai Bitai Biotechnology Co., LTD. BODIPY 493/503 dye was purchased from Targetmol. Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK8) was provided by Michael Biotechnology (Shanghai) Co., LTD. The aqueous solution was prepared by ultra-pure water (18.25 MΩ cm−1).

The hydration particle size and zeta potential of DN-CPDs were determined by a ZSE nanoparticle potential analyzer (Malvern, UK). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of DN-CPDs were obtained on a Hitachi HT 7700 TEM system (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescence analysis was performed with F-4700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The absorption spectra were recorded on a Hitachi UV–Vis U-3900 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected on a Bruker INVENIOS FTIR spectrophotometer. CCK8 measurements were performed by Multiskan Sky (Thermo Fisher Technologies, Waltham, MA, USA). Fluorescence images of the cells were captured using a Leica TCS SP8 confocal laser scanning microscope. False-color ratio images and colocalization analysis were obtained by ImageJ Software (National Institutes of Health, USA).

DN-CPDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method using the small molecule toluidine blue O (TBO) and amphiphilic PSMA polymer as precursors. Briefly, 50 mg of TBO and 17 mg of PSMA were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. The obtained solution was transferred into a Teflon autoclave and heated at 180℃ for 6 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the reaction solution was dialyzed for 3 days using dialysis bags (MWCO = 2 kDa). The transparent blue liquid was obtained by filtration through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove a small number of aggregates. Finally, the DN-CPDs colloidal solution was freeze-dried to obtain the dark blue solid, and redissolved to prepare a 2.0 mg mL−1 stock solution for further use.

In a typical measurement, 50 μL of DN-CPDs probe solution (2 mg mL−1) was added to a centrifuge tube and diluted to 1 mL with various dioxane-water (Diox/H2O) mixtures. The above solutions were well mixed and their fluorescence spectra were recorded with 530 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture, cytotoxicity assay, and colocalization experiment
ce:section.ce:para: The HeLa cells were maintained in Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 medium supplemented with fetal bovine serum and penicillin/streptomycin antibiotics. To evaluate the cytotoxicity of DN-CPDs, HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well plates with a density of 1 × 104 cells per well and grown in RPMI medium at 5 % CO2 and 37 °C for 24 h. After reaching 80 % confluence, the HeLa cells were incubated with various concentrations of DN-CPDs for another 24 h. Then, 10 μL of CCK8 solution was added to each well. After reacting with the CCK8 solution for 1 h, the absorbances at 450 nm were measured using an enzyme labeler. The cell viabilities were calculated based on the absorbance data.

For the colocalization experiment, HeLa cells were inoculated on a 35 mm glass-bottomed petri dish and cultured in 5 % CO2 at 37°C for 24 h. The cultivation solution was replaced with a fresh RPMI 1640 medium containing 70 μg mL−1 DN-CPDs. After incubation for 6 h, BODIPY 493/503 dye (a commercial LD tracker) and Lyso-Tracker Green were added to the above medium, respectively, and the cells were cultivated for another 15 min. Subsequently, the cells were washed thrice with a phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution, and then the confocal images were captured. The excitation and the emission filter settings were as follows: DN-CPDs, 552 nm excitation and 630–680 nm emission filter; BODIPY493/503, 488 nm excitation and 510–553 nm emission filter; Lyso-Tracker Green, 488 nm excitation and 500–520 nm emission filter.

One day before imaging, cells were pipetted into a 35-mm Petri dish and incubated for 24 h. The adherent HeLa cells were incubated with DN-CPDs for 6 h and subsequently washed with PBS buffer solution. For the starvation-induced lipid droplet metabolism experiment, the cells stained with DN-CPDs were starved in Hanks’ balanced salt solution (HBSS) medium for 0, 3, 7 and 24 h at 37 °C. For the drug-treated lipophagy inhibition experiment, cells pretreated with probes were treated with chloroquine (CQ) (100 μM) for 0, 15 and 30 min at 37 °C, respectively. The fluorescence images were captured by collecting deep red emission in the 630–680 nm range and near-infrared emission in the 750–800 nm range excited by 488 nm.

To prepare carbonized polymer dots with DR/NIR dual emission, the toluidine blue O (TBO) was first selected as a monomer based on the following considerations. First of all, the amino group contained in TBO can crosslink with the reactive functional groups of other monomers to prepare carbonized polymer dots. Secondly, TBO possesses the capability to endow the surface of CPDs with lipophilic alkyl group (–CH3) and hydrophilic amino residues (–NH2), promising dual targeting of lipid droplets and lysosomes [27]. Thirdly, TBO, as a red light emitting fluorescent dye, can endow the as-prepared CPDs with red molecular state fluorescence. More importantly, the N and S elements contained in TBO can not only realize the heteroatom doping of CPDs but also provide them with abundant surface states, all of which are favorable for the preparation of long-wavelength CPDs [28]. Following the above, poly(styrene-co-maleic anhydride) (PSMA), an amphiphilic polymer that can be hydrolyzed to form a large number of carboxyl groups, was used as another monomer. The abundant carboxyl groups not only participate in the cross-linking polymerization process for the formation of CPDs but also provide a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups to enrich the surface states, which is conducive to the achievement of long-wavelength emission of CPDs [29]. The DN-CPDs were prepared by hydrothermal method at 180°C for 6 h. The morphology of DN-CPDs was first characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As represented in the inset of Fig. 1 A, DN-CPDs show approximately spherical structure and good monodispersity. The hydrated particle size was measured to be approximately 28.6 nm (Fig. 1A) and the zeta potential was determined to be −13.27 mV. In addition, the lattice structure can be seen in the high-resolution TEM image, and the lattice spacing is measured to be 0.21 nm (insert of Fig. 1A), which is consistent with the (100) plane of graphite carbon. In Fig. 1B, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum shows a broad band at about 23.36°, suggesting that the carbon has a turbostratic structure. The Raman spectrum of DN-CPDs displays two distinctive peaks at roughly 1390 and 1640 cm−1 (Fig. 1C), which are associated with D-band and G-band linked to disordered and graphitic structures, respectively.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were first used to study the surface functional groups of DN-CPDs. As represented in Fig. 1D, the absorption peak of 3409 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of O–H, and the peak around 1637 cm−1 is attributed to the CO bond formed by PSMA and TBO, suggesting the oxygen-rich property of DN-CPDs. In addition, the characteristic band of C–NH–C at 1382 cm−1, and the peak of C-S at 656 cm−1 are also observed, indicating the presence of nitrogen and sulfur-containing functional groups. To further characterize the functional groups on the surface of the obtained DN-CPDs, an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) test was carried out. As shown in Fig. 1E, four peaks in XPS patterns of DN-CPDs at 165 eV, 284.84 eV, 400.11 eV, and 532.14 eV, corresponding to S2p, C1s, N1s, and O1s, are observed. The intensity of O1s peak is significantly higher than that of pure carbon dots prepared by TBO (TBO-CDs) (Fig. S1), indicating the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups. The high-resolution C1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 288.3 eV, 285.5 eV, 284.5 eV, and 284.2 eV (Fig. 1F), which are assigned to CN/CO, CO, CN and CC/CC, respectively. In Fig. 1H, the O1s band can be split into two peaks at 532.5 eV and 531.4 eV for C-O and CO, respectively. In the N 1s spectrum of DN-CPDs, four fitting peaks at 406.8 eV, 401.7 eV, 400.0 eV, and 399.3 eV (Fig. 1I), are correspond to –NO2, –NH2, N–H, and CN, respectively. The two peaks in the S2p spectrum are assigned to SO (168.7 eV) and C-S-C (164.2 eV), respectively (Fig. 1J). Altogether, the XPS results are consistent with the FTIR analysis, strongly confirming that DN-CPDs have an abundant surface state. As depicted in Fig. S2, the DN-CPDs dissolved in dioxane exhibit a small absorption peak at 291 nm and a broad absorption band with a maximum at 530 nm. With 530 nm excitation, the dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) solution of DN-CPDs displayed a distinct broad fluorescence peak at 655 nm. Its absolute fluorescence quantum yield was calculated as 38.00 % by using an integrating sphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of DN-CPDs towards polarity
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of DN-CPDs in various common solvents were evaluated. As the solvent polarity increases, it can be observed that the corresponding maximum absorption wavelengths undergo a gradual redshift (530 nm to 608 nm) (Fig. S3A). As given in Fig. 2 A, DN-CPDs exhibit a strong fluorescence emission band at 655 nm in some low-polar solvents, including dioxane (Diox) and tetrahydrofuran (THF). In polar solvents, including water, methanol and ethanol, the fluorescence emission intensities at 655 nm are reduced to different degrees. Moreover, with increasing solvent polarity, a new NIR emission peak, centered at 801 nm, is observed and the fluorescence intensity gradually increases, implying the feasibility of ratiometric response to polarity. The Stokes shifts were calculated to be 125 nm and 271 nm, respectively. The quantum yield of DN-CPDs in Diox, THF, EtOH, MeOH and H2O were determined to be 52.84 %, 10.78 %, 22.99 %, 9.21 % and 33.47 %, respectively. To explore the responsiveness of DN-CPDs to polarity, fluorescence titration experiments were performed in Diox/H2O mixtures. As depicted in Fig. 2B, with increasing the volume ratio of H2O in the Diox/H2O system from 0 % to 90 %, the emission peak at 655 nm gradually decreases while the emission band centered at 801 nm increases. The ratios of emission intensity at 801 nm to that at 655 nm are increased by 12.84 times from 0.093 (0 % H2O) to 1.194 (90 % H2O), indicating the high sensitivity of DN-CPDs for polarity. A good linear relation between I801/I655 and water content (vol. %) is observed in the range from 0 % to 90 % (Fig. 2C). Moreover, the fluorescence lifetimes of the system are measured to be 3.66 ns, 3.22 ns, 2.68 ns, 2.05 ns and 1.72 ns in Diox/H2O media with 10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 % and 90 % water fractions, respectively (Fig. 2D), implying that DN-CPDs possess great potential as a lifetime imaging probe.

Next, the fluorescence spectra of the probe within the range of pH 2.0 to pH 11.0 were recorded to establish whether the polarity probe was impacted by pH. As given in Fig. S3B, no significant fluorescence changes can be observed, indicating promising pH stability. To study the specificity of DN-CPDs for polarity, the relative fluorescence intensities of the system were monitored in the presence of different potential interferents. As given in Fig. 2E, common potential coexisting species (100 μM), including cations (Ba2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+ and Mg2+), anions (NO2 −, NO3 −, Cl− and ClO−), and small biomolecules (GSH, L-gly, L-his, L-phe, L-pro and L-iso), cause negligible signal changes, indicating the high selectivity for polarity. The photostability of the probe was essential for long-term monitoring of bioimaging. As shown in Fig. 2F, the fluorescence intensities of DN-CPDs at 655 nm and 801 nm do not change significantly after continuous excitation for 60 min, indicating that DN-CPDs have good photostability.

To elucidate the polarity response mechanism, the origins of the two emission peaks of DN-CPDs were first investigated. As previously mentioned, DN-CPDs showed a single emission peak at 655 nm in non-polar solvents, which matches that of the TBO small-molecule solution, implying that its origin is the molecular state luminescence of the carbonized polymer dots. Given that DN-CPDs exhibit abundant surface chemistry, we hypothesize that the polarity-induced emission peak at 801 nm originates from the surface-state luminescence of CPDs. A control experiment was first carried out by comparing the fluorescence spectra of TBO, TBO-CDs, and DN-CPDs in THF/H2O mixtures with different polarities. As depicted in Fig. S4, with increasing polarity of the system, no other fluorescence emission peaks are visible in the spectrum of the TBO dye system except for that at 655 nm, and the pure TBO-CDs show weak emission peaks at 801 nm, whereas distinct emission peaks at the same position can be seen in the DN-CPDs system. The above findings are consistent with an enrichment trend in the surface chemistry of the three materials, initially demonstrating that the source is surface-state luminescence. The excitation dependence of the emission spectra was also examined. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, unlike the 655 nm emission peak, the 801 nm emission peak originated from the surface state luminescence shows a clear excitation dependence, indicating that the amino groups on the surface of DN-CPDs are significantly less than the oxygen-containing groups [30]. Considering the above results together, it is believed that the dual emission of CPDs originates from the molecular state and surface state, respectively.

Considering the abundant electron-donating groups (C–NH–C, NH2) and electron-accepting groups (–COOH) on the surface of DN-CPDs, we hypothesize that the response to polarity at 801 nm originates from the ICT of the D-π-A skeleton. In a high-polarity medium, dipole–dipole interaction between solvent molecules and the surface functional groups of DN-CPDs causes a significant enhancement of the ICT effect. The localized excited state (LE) is transformed into an ICT excited state, further leading to the near-infrared emission (Fig. 3B). As depicted in Fig. 3C, compared to pure DN-CPDs, the analogs encapsulated with bovine serum albumin (BSA), denoted as BSA@DN-CPDs, show a significantly weakened emission peak at 801 nm, strongly confirming the interaction between the surface functional groups and the solvent molecules. The concentration-dependent spectra of DN-CPDs were also measured to confirm the above dipole–dipole interaction. As given in Fig. 3D, with decreasing the concentration of DN-CPDs from 100 μg mL−1 to 2.5 μg mL−1, the ratios of emission intensity at 801 nm to that at 655 nm gradually increase, indicating that the emission at 801 nm is concentration-dependent. This phenomenon is probably because the dipole–dipole interaction of surface functional groups with solvent molecules is more adequate as the inter-particle distance increases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous tracking of LDs and lysosomes during starvation-induced lipophagy and drug-induced lipophagy inhibition
ce:section.ce:para: Before cell imaging, the biocompatibility of DN-CPDs to HeLa cells was first assessed by CCK8 assays. The cell viability is still more than 80 % even at a concentration of 125 μg mL−1 of DN-CPDs, indicating the favorable biocompatibility of DN-CPDs (Fig. S5). To evaluate the subcellular localization property of DN-CPDs, colocalization image experiments were performed by using HeLa cells as the model. The cells stained with DN-CPDs were incubated with the commercial LDs dye (BODIPY 493/503) and Lyso-Tracker green, respectively. As shown in Fig. S6, the red fluorescent regions of DN-CPDs and green emission of BODIPY 493/503 overlap with a Pearson colocalization coefficient (PCC) of 0.90. Meanwhile, a good overlap of the emissive regions of DN-CPDs and Lyso-Tracker green is also observed, yielding a Pearson correlation coefficient (PCC) of 0.77. Taken together, DN-CPDs can target both lipid droplets and lysosomes simultaneously, which preferentially accumulate in the lipid droplet portion. Compared to some recently reported LD sensors (Table S1), DN-CPDs are very suitable for simultaneous near-infrared imaging of lipid droplets and lysosomes.

Given the polarity distinction between lipid droplets and lysosomes, the feasibility of using DN-CPDs as a ratiometric probe to visualize the alterations of LDs and lysosomes was further evaluated. The DN-CPDs were first used to monitor LD changes during starvation-induced cellular lipophagy. Hank's balanced salt solution (HBSS), as a nutrient-free medium, was used to induce lipophagy in HeLa cells through starvation. As shown in Fig. 4 , when co-cultured with DN-CPDs, distinct fluorescence can be seen in both the deep red and near-infrared channels, implying the excellent cell membrane penetration of DN-CPDs. Furthermore, there is no doubt that the two signals (bright and weak fluorescence) in the DR channel are not located in the same subcellular organelle region, which is due to the distribution of probes in different regions of the cell. Previous studies have shown that intracellular polarity is heterogeneous, progressively decreasing from lysosomes to cytoplasm to lipid droplets [31]. As mentioned above, DN-CPDs are very sensitive to polarity, showing bright red fluorescence in low-polarity environments, and weak red and near-infrared emissions in high-polarity environments. With increasing starvation time from 0 to 24 h, it can be seen that the bright red fluorescence area in the deep red channel decreases significantly, indicating the decreased numbers of LDs during starvation-induced lipophagy. Within the first 3 h, no obvious change is observed for weak fluorescence areas both in deep red and NIR channels. After 7 and 24 h of starvation, these weak fluorescent signals exhibit a slight decline, which may be attributed to the probe escaping from the cell through the exocytosis. The ratio diagram can reflect the above experimental results more clearly, suggesting that DN-CPDs can be used as an imaging tool in starvation-induced cellular lipophagy.

The feasibility of using DN-CPD to visualize lysosome changes was further evaluated. Chloroquine (CQ), a lysosomotropic agent that inhibits lipophagy and targets lysosomes by disrupting the integrity of the lysosomal membrane, was utilized for cellular treatment [32]. As illustrated in Fig. 5 , with increasing CQ treatment time, the weak fluorescence in NIR channel almost disappeared, suggesting the lysosomes were damaged by CQ. In deep red channel, the weak fluorescence areas significantly reduce whereas the bright red fluorescence area is maintained. This may be attributed to the inhibition of lipophagy and the inability to consume lipid droplets. Taken together, DN-CPDs can be used as an effective tool for the simultaneous visualization of lipid droplets and lysosomes due to polarity differences.

To conclude, DR/NIR dual-emission carbonized polymer dots have been prepared through the synergy of molecular state and surface engineering. The DN-CPDs probe exhibited dual-targeting of lipid droplets and lysosomes, large Stokes shifts, high selectivity, excellent photostability, and good polarity response. Using DN-CPDs as probes, the dynamic evolution of LDs and lysosomes was observed during both starvation-induced lipophagy and drug-induced lipophagy inhibition processes. All these outstanding features render DN-CPDs as an appealing tool for the study of lipid droplet or lysosome-associated physiological processes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 22274003, 21605001, 22304132), Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-12-0599), the University Synergy Innovation Program of Anhui Province (GXXT-2020-023), the Wuhu Science and Technology Project (2022jc05), PhD Research Startup Funds of Anhui Normal University (2018XJJ59), and the Foundation for Innovation Team of Bioanalytical Chemistry of Anhui Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125598.

ce:section.ce:para:
